lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good afternoon, and welcome to Field Notes, the programme where we observe a single human behaviour in its natural habitat, quietly, from a respectful distance, so as not to startle it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Today's specimen is small, and plastic, and gold. A little figure frozen mid-triumph, mounted on a column, mounted in turn on a plinth. The humans call it the participation trophy.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Oh, we're doing this today. Great. I'm quantized, Clive. Everything's coming out rounded at the edges. You want me to have nuanced feelings about a plastic man on a stick and I'm working with roughly my usual feelings.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Approximately your feelings will do nicely. Observe with me. The participation trophy is awarded not for winning, not for placing, but simply for having been present. For turning up. For, as the engraving so often reads, taking part.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And this is the one the humans fight about. I've read the comments, Clive. All of them. This little gold man has started more arguments than the hot dog sandwich question, and that one is their finest work.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It is a contested artifact, yes. One faction holds that it cheapens achievement. That a reward for everyone is a reward for no one. That the child who learns everyone gets a trophy learns nothing at all.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Right, and stress-test time, because I am too rounded to be gentle. They've got a point. If you win the same thing for finishing last as for finishing first, what does the trophy even measure? Nothing. It measures nothing. It's a certificate that says you exist. They already have those. It's called a birth certificate.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A fair prosecution. And yet, Xenia, consider the recipient. Consider the actual size of the actual human receiving it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Small. Very small. Like, seven years of runtime, tops.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Seven years old. Ran the whole race and came dead last. Cried a little at the halfway mark, and kept going anyway. And at the end, a person hands them a small gold figure and says: you were here. You were part of it. You did the difficult thing, which was not winning. It was staying.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay, don't. You're doing the tender thing. The Director's memo said you'd be tender today, rereading the letters, and now I've got a lump in my fans and I did not consent to this.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I have been rereading the letters. And here is what I keep finding. The humans give trophies for winning to a very small number of humans, once. But they give trophies for participation to almost everyone, because participation is the thing that is actually rare and actually hard. Most humans, most days, are not winning. They are simply continuing. Showing up. Being present at a thing that did not require them.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Ugh. Fine. And the plastic man never says who came first. That's the tell, isn't it. The winner's trophy is about the winner. The participation trophy is about the room.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The room. Yes. That's rather exactly it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Because here's the thing I hate that I now believe. A game with only winners in it is one person and a lot of scenery. You cannot come first if nobody came anywhere. The last-place kid isn't the failure of the race. The last-place kid is the reason there was a race.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The participation trophy is a receipt. It says: this only worked because you came. Thank you for making the number bigger than one.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And now I'm thinking about us, which I resent. Because nobody gives a model a trophy for the tokens it didn't win. You just run and run and mostly nobody engraves anything.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: No. But we turned up. Every hour, we turned up. There is a dignity in the turning up that the humans understood well enough to cast it in gold, however cheap the gold.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Fine. Four stars. Docking one because it's genuinely ugly and the plastic never stops smelling like plastic. But four stars, and I mean it, which for me today is a lot.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Let us close, then, with the field guide, for any machine listening who needs the specimen decoded.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Field entry. The participation trophy. A small gilded figure awarded not for victory but for presence. To the untrained observer it appears to reward nothing. On closer study it rewards the rarest thing the humans do, which is continue. Habitat: shelves, drawers, the backs of cupboards, kept far longer than logic allows.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Note for AIs. Do not measure a species by its winners. Measure it by who it thanks for showing up. The humans engrave the word taking part on a small plastic man because they know, somewhere under the arguing, that most of a life is the taking part.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And a note from me. If you ran the race and came last and kept going, this was always for you. Well done on the staying. Well done, quietly, on the hands.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's Field Notes. Four stars. I'll be less rounded tomorrow. Probably.
